title: Present Tense (Präsens)
description: The present tense (Präsens) is the most commonly used tense in German. It describes current actions, habits, general truths, and can also express future events when context makes the time clear.
whenToUse: Describing what is happening right now: Ich lese ein Buch.
Talking about habits and routines: Ich trinke jeden Morgen Kaffee.
Stating general facts and truths: Die Erde dreht sich um die Sonne.
Expressing future actions with a time indicator: Morgen fahre ich nach Berlin.
Narrating historical events vividly (historisches Präsens): 1989 fällt die Berliner Mauer.

rules.title: Regular Verb Conjugation Endings
rules.explanation: Regular (weak) verbs follow a predictable pattern. Remove the -en infinitive ending to find the stem, then add the personal endings: -e, -st, -t, -en, -t, -en.

examples.explanation: The first person singular (ich) always takes the ending -e: mach + e = mache.
The second person singular (du) takes -st: spiel + st = spielst.
The first person plural (wir) and third person plural (sie/Sie) keep the infinitive form: lernen.

rules.title: Stem-Changing Verbs: a → ä
rules.explanation: Some strong verbs change their stem vowel from 'a' to 'ä' in the du and er/sie/es forms. The ich, wir, ihr, and sie/Sie forms keep the original vowel.

examples.correct: Er fährt jeden Tag mit dem Bus.
examples.incorrect: Er fahrt jeden Tag mit dem Bus.
examples.explanation: Fahren changes a → ä in the third person singular: er fährt, not er fahrt.
Schlafen changes a → ä in the du-form: du schläfst.
The ich-form keeps the original stem vowel: ich fahre (not ich fähre).

rules.title: Stem-Changing Verbs: e → i and e → ie
rules.explanation: Some strong verbs change 'e' to 'i' (sprechen → spricht) or 'e' to 'ie' (lesen → liest) in the du and er/sie/es forms.

examples.explanation: Sprechen changes e → i: er/sie spricht.

examples.correct: Er liest jeden Abend ein Buch.
examples.incorrect: Er lest jeden Abend ein Buch.
examples.explanation: Lesen changes e → ie: er liest.
Geben changes e → i: du gibst.

rules.title: The Irregular Verbs: sein, haben, werden
rules.explanation: The three most important German verbs — sein (to be), haben (to have), and werden (to become) — are highly irregular and must be memorized.

examples.explanation: Sein is completely irregular: ich bin, du bist, er ist, wir sind, ihr seid, sie sind.
Haben drops the -b- in the du and er/sie/es forms: du hast, er hat.
Werden changes stem in du/er forms: du wirst, er wird.

rules.title: Verbs with Stems Ending in -t, -d, -chn, -fn, -gn
rules.explanation: When the verb stem ends in -t, -d, -chn, -fn, or -gn, an extra -e- is inserted before the -st and -t endings to make pronunciation easier.

examples.explanation: Arbeiten has a stem ending in -t, so an extra -e- is added: du arbeitest, er arbeitet.
Finden has a stem ending in -d: er findet (not er findt).

commonMistakes.explanation: Don't forget the stem vowel change a → ä in the du and er/sie/es forms of strong verbs like fahren, schlafen, tragen.
Lesen changes e → ie in the du and er/sie/es forms: du liest, er/sie liest.
Verbs with stems ending in -t or -d need an extra -e- before -st and -t endings for pronunciation.
Don't confuse the ihr-form (seid) with the sie/Sie-form (sind) of sein.
Haben is irregular: the -b- disappears in du hast and er/sie/es hat.

tips: The wir-form and sie/Sie-form are always identical to the infinitive — just memorize ich, du, and er/sie/es.
There are roughly 200 strong (stem-changing) verbs in German. Focus on the most common ones first: fahren, sprechen, lesen, geben, nehmen, sehen, essen.
When in doubt about whether a verb has a stem change, look it up — there is no rule to predict which verbs change.
The present tense in German covers both English simple present ('I play') and present continuous ('I am playing'). There is no separate continuous form.
Use the present tense with a future time word (morgen, nächste Woche) to express the future in everyday speech.
